display_name: Wilmington, North Carolina (Cape Fear Port District)

neighborhoods.name: Downtown / Riverfront District
primary_groups: White merchants, railroad workers, and professionals; mixed-race commercial district before 1898
neighborhoods.character: Wilmington's downtown hugged the Cape Fear River — the wharves, cotton warehouses, naval stores yards, and railroad terminals clustered along Water Street and the riverfront. Before 1898, downtown Wilmington was one of the most racially integrated commercial districts in the South, with Black and white businesses operating in close proximity and a biracial city government that functioned effectively. The 1898 coup destroyed this arrangement — white supremacists overthrew the elected government, drove Black business owners from their properties, and imposed rigid racial segregation that lasted for decades. After 1898, the downtown became almost exclusively white-controlled, though Black workers continued to labor on the docks and in the warehouses.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Wilmington's riverfront was where cotton, lumber, and naval stores met the ships that carried them to the world — if {ancestor_name} worked the downtown wharves or warehouses in the {decade}s, the {surname} family was part of a port economy that made Wilmington the largest city in North Carolina until the twentieth century.

neighborhoods.name: Brooklyn (Black Community)
primary_groups: African American families — freed people, their descendants, and Black migrants from rural southeastern NC counties
neighborhoods.character: Brooklyn was Wilmington's largest and most established Black neighborhood — a thriving community of homes, churches, schools, and Black-owned businesses that stretched north of downtown. Before 1898, Brooklyn's residents included prosperous Black professionals, skilled artisans, and political leaders who participated in Wilmington's biracial government. The 1898 massacre devastated the community — armed white mobs invaded Brooklyn, killed residents (estimates range from dozens to over 100), and drove Black leaders from the city. Brooklyn survived but under the crushing weight of Jim Crow. In the 1960s-1970s, urban renewal demolished much of what remained of historic Brooklyn, displacing families who had lived there for generations.
key_institutions: Williston Industrial High School (Black school, later Williston Senior High)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Brooklyn was the heart of Black Wilmington — if {ancestor_name} lived here, the {surname} family was part of a community that survived the 1898 massacre, endured Jim Crow, and then watched the bulldozers of urban renewal finish what the white mob had started. The records that survive — church rolls, school registers, property deeds — document a community's extraordinary resilience.

neighborhoods.name: Dry Pond
primary_groups: White working-class families — railroad workers, dock laborers, small tradesmen
neighborhoods.character: Dry Pond was a white working-class neighborhood on the north side of Wilmington, named for a low-lying area that flooded in wet weather and dried to cracked mud in summer. The neighborhood was home to Atlantic Coast Line railroad workers, dock hands, and the laboring families who kept Wilmington's port economy running. Dry Pond was rougher and poorer than the genteel neighborhoods south of Market Street — a place of boarding houses, small frame cottages, and the saloons and jook joints that served the waterfront workforce. During the 1898 events, Dry Pond's white residents were among the foot soldiers of the mob.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Dry Pond was working Wilmington — if {ancestor_name} lived in this neighborhood in the {decade}s, the {surname} family was part of the white laboring class that loaded the ships, maintained the railroad, and lived in the shadow of both the cotton barons' mansions and the racial violence that reshaped the city in 1898.

neighborhoods.name: Carolina Beach Road / South Wilmington
primary_groups: White working-class and lower-middle-class families; expanding residential area as Wilmington grew southward
neighborhoods.character: As Wilmington expanded in the early twentieth century, residential development pushed south along the road to Carolina Beach. This area mixed older farming land with new working-class subdivisions, attracting families from both the city and the surrounding rural counties. The neighborhood was less defined by a single employer or industry than the downtown or Dry Pond — residents worked at the railroad, the port, local businesses, and increasingly at Camp Davis (WWII-era military installation) and the North Carolina Shipbuilding Company during wartime.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: South Wilmington was where the city was growing — if {ancestor_name} settled along Carolina Beach Road in the {decade}s, the {surname} family was part of Wilmington's expansion beyond its old riverfront core, in neighborhoods that mixed city workers with families still connected to the rural Cape Fear countryside.

neighborhoods.name: Castle Street Corridor
primary_groups: Mixed — white residential neighborhoods intersecting with Black commercial areas; complex racial geography
neighborhoods.character: Castle Street ran through a section of Wilmington where white and Black neighborhoods existed in close but carefully maintained proximity — a pattern common in Southern port cities. The corridor included modest white residential blocks, segments of Black-owned commercial businesses, and the edges of the Brooklyn community. Before 1898, this proximity functioned with relative ease; after the coup, the racial boundaries hardened and the social geography became rigidly enforced. Castle Street's mixed character makes it genealogically important — families of both races appear in the property records and city directories for this area.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Castle Street was where Wilmington's racial geography was most visible — if {ancestor_name} lived or worked along this corridor, the {surname} family inhabited a space where Black and white Wilmington existed in proximity that the 1898 coup tried to destroy but could not entirely erase.

major_employers.name: Atlantic Coast Line Railroad
major_employers.wages: Railroad wages were among the best available in Wilmington. Engineers earned $3-5/day by the 1900s; skilled shop workers $2-3/day; laborers $1-1.50/day. The ACL was one of the few employers that offered career progression — a fireman could work toward engineer, a shop helper toward mechanic. Railroad employment provided more stability than dock work, which was seasonal and weather-dependent.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Atlantic Coast Line Railroad headquartered in Wilmington from 1869 and was the city's dominant employer for nearly a century. The ACL connected Wilmington to Richmond, Charleston, and Florida, carrying cotton, lumber, and naval stores to the port and bringing manufactured goods inland. The railroad shops employed skilled mechanics in locomotive repair and maintenance. Railroad work was dangerous — coupling accidents, boiler explosions, and derailments killed and maimed workers regularly. The Brotherhood of Locomotive Engineers and other railroad unions established early in Wilmington, giving railroad workers more organized protection than most Southern laborers.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The Atlantic Coast Line was Wilmington's spine — if {ancestor_name} worked for the ACL, the {surname} family drew wages from the railroad that connected the Cape Fear to the rest of the Eastern Seaboard. The ACL's headquarters made Wilmington a railroad town as much as a port town, and the payroll kept the city alive.

major_employers.name: Port of Wilmington / Stevedoring and Shipping
major_employers.wages: Dock work paid by the day or the load — $1-2/day for common labor in the 1880s-1900s, with experienced stevedores earning more. Work was irregular — dependent on ship arrivals, weather, and seasonal cotton and lumber shipments. Black workers dominated the stevedoring workforce, performing the heaviest and most dangerous work for the lowest pay.
major_employers.working_conditions: Wilmington's port handled cotton, lumber, naval stores (turpentine, rosin, tar), and later petroleum products. Stevedoring was brutally physical — loading cotton bales, lumber, and barrels by hand onto sailing vessels and later steamships. The work was racially stratified — Black longshoremen did the heavy lifting while white workers held the better-paying skilled positions and supervisory roles. The naval stores trade (turpentine and rosin from the longleaf pine forests) was a distinctive feature of Wilmington's port economy, connecting the city to the rural turpentine camps of southeastern North Carolina.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The wharves were where Wilmington earned its money — if {ancestor_name} worked the docks, the {surname} family was part of a labor force that loaded Cape Fear cotton and longleaf pine naval stores onto ships bound for Liverpool and New York, in work that was backbreaking, irregular, and divided sharply along racial lines.

major_employers.name: Sprunt Cotton Export Company
major_employers.wages: Sprunt was the largest cotton export operation in the South — Alexander Sprunt & Son handled more cotton through Wilmington than any other firm. Warehouse and compress workers earned standard dock wages ($1-2/day); clerks and samplers earned more.
major_employers.working_conditions: Alexander Sprunt, a Scottish immigrant, built the largest cotton export business in the American South from his Wilmington base. The Sprunt company operated massive cotton compresses that squeezed bales to half their size for efficient shipping, and maintained warehouses along the riverfront that stored thousands of bales awaiting export. The cotton trade was seasonal — the busiest months were fall through early spring, when the crop moved from inland farms to the port. Sprunt's dominance of the cotton trade made the company one of Wilmington's most powerful institutions.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Sprunt moved more cotton through Wilmington than anyone — if {ancestor_name} worked in the compress or the warehouse, the {surname} family was part of a Scottish-run enterprise that turned the Cape Fear River into a cotton highway connecting Carolina plantations to European textile mills.

major_employers.name: North Carolina Shipbuilding Company (WWII)
major_employers.wages: Wartime shipyard wages were far above pre-war Wilmington standards — skilled workers earned $1.00-1.50/hour, unskilled $0.60-0.80/hour. The shipyard employed over 20,000 workers at peak production, transforming Wilmington's economy overnight and drawing workers from across eastern North Carolina and beyond.
major_employers.working_conditions: The North Carolina Shipbuilding Company built 243 Liberty ships and other vessels for the war effort between 1941 and 1946, making Wilmington one of the most important shipbuilding centers on the East Coast. The shipyard was the largest employer in Wilmington's history, hiring workers regardless of previous experience and training them on the job. Women and Black workers entered industrial employment in unprecedented numbers, though segregation persisted in job assignments and facilities. When the shipyard closed after the war, the sudden loss of 20,000 jobs devastated Wilmington's economy.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The shipyard changed everything — if {ancestor_name} welded Liberty ships during the war, the {surname} family earned more money in the North Carolina Shipbuilding Company than the Cape Fear had ever paid, in a wartime boom that pulled 20,000 workers to Wilmington and then vanished when the last ship launched.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Wilmington's housing patterns were shaped by the port economy and by the racial geography of a Southern city. White workers rented modest frame houses in neighborhoods like Dry Pond or along the railroad corridors. Black families lived in Brooklyn and other designated Black neighborhoods, in housing that ranged from the substantial homes of the Black middle class to the cramped rental properties of the laboring poor. Boarding houses were common near the waterfront for single workers and new arrivals. After the 1898 coup, housing segregation intensified and the boundaries of Black neighborhoods were more rigidly enforced.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Racial rather than ethnic — Wilmington's population was primarily native-born Black and white, with a small but significant immigrant population (German, Jewish, Greek, Lebanese merchants and tradesmen). The critical division was racial. Before 1898, Black neighborhoods like Brooklyn were prosperous and politically empowered; after 1898, they were contained, diminished, and excluded from political participation. White neighborhoods ranged from the grand homes of the cotton merchants and railroad executives south of Market Street to the working-class cottages of Dry Pond and the railroad corridors.
housing_patterns.trajectory: For white families: boarding house or rental (months to years) to modest homeownership in working-class neighborhoods (5-15 years), with upward mobility possible through railroad employment or skilled trades. For Black families: rental housing in Brooklyn or other Black neighborhoods (often for generations, as racial covenants and economic exclusion limited mobility) to homeownership within Black neighborhoods. The 1898 massacre destroyed Black wealth and political power, setting back homeownership and economic advancement for generations. Urban renewal in the 1960s-1970s demolished much of Brooklyn, displacing established homeowners.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Wilmington's defining episode of racial violence was the 1898 Wilmington Massacre and Coup — the only successful coup d'etat in American history, in which armed white supremacists overthrew the elected biracial city government, murdered Black residents, and installed a white supremacist regime. This event is covered in detail by the separate trigger file `nc_wilmington_1898_exodus`. The 1898 events were not spontaneous — they were planned and executed by Wilmington's white elite, including business leaders, editors, and politicians who used racial terrorism to seize permanent political control.
specific_incidents: 1898 Wilmington Massacre and Coup (November 10, 1898) — Armed white mobs, led by former Confederate officer Alfred Moore Waddell and organized by the Wilmington business elite, attacked the Black community, burned the offices of Alexander Manly's Daily Record (a Black newspaper), killed an unknown number of Black residents (estimates range from dozens to over 100), and forced the elected mayor, board of aldermen, and police chief to resign at gunpoint. Waddell installed himself as mayor. Over 2,000 Black residents fled Wilmington permanently. This was the foundational event in North Carolina's white supremacy campaign that disfranchised Black voters statewide by 1900.

1971 Wilmington Ten case — ten civil rights activists (nine Black men and one white woman) were convicted on arson and conspiracy charges related to racial unrest in Wilmington. The convictions were widely regarded as politically motivated, and the case became an international human rights cause. All convictions were overturned in 1980, and pardons of innocence were granted in 2012.
nativist_organizations: Ku Klux Klan (active in New Hanover County in the 1920s and during the civil rights era)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Stephen AME Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community in Brooklyn and greater Wilmington
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptismal records, marriage records. St. Stephen was one of the anchor institutions of Black Wilmington, surviving the 1898 massacre and maintaining community records through Jim Crow. Church records document family relationships and vital events for the Black community across generations.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: St. Stephen AME held Black Wilmington together through the worst — if {ancestor_name} worshipped here, the {surname} family was part of a congregation that survived the 1898 massacre, endured Jim Crow, and kept records that document a community the rest of the city tried to erase.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: First Baptist Church of Wilmington
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptismal registers, marriage records. First Baptist was one of the leading white churches in Wilmington and its records cover the city's white population across the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} appears in the records of First Baptist, the {surname} family was part of Wilmington's white establishment — the church that anchored the community of merchants, railroad men, and professionals who built the city's economy on cotton and shipping.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: New Hanover County Register of Deeds and Court Records
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: All residents of New Hanover County
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Deed books, marriage registers, estate files, tax records, court minutes. New Hanover County records are among the oldest and most complete in North Carolina, with deed records extending to the colonial era. For post-1898 research, property records document the transfer of Black-owned property to white buyers under duress — deeds executed in the weeks after the massacre may reflect forced sales. The NC State Archives holds microfilmed copies, and the New Hanover County Public Library maintains an extensive local history collection.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: New Hanover County's records go back to 1729 — if {ancestor_name} lived in Wilmington, the deed books, marriage registers, and court minutes document the {surname} family across nearly three centuries. But read the records carefully — a property deed signed in November 1898 may tell a story of violence, not voluntary sale.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Williston Industrial High School Records
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Student records, yearbooks, alumni records. Williston was the primary Black high school in Wilmington and a center of the African American community for nearly a century. Its alumni records and yearbooks document generations of Black families and provide biographical information not available in other sources. Williston was closed during school desegregation in 1968.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Williston was more than a school — it was the institution that educated, connected, and sustained Black Wilmington for almost a century. If {ancestor_name}'s children or grandchildren attended Williston, the yearbooks and alumni records preserve faces and names that the rest of the city's records too often ignored.

source_references.author: David S. Cecelski and Timothy B. Tyson, editors
source_references.title: Democracy Betrayed: The Wilmington Race Riot of 1898 and Its Legacy
source_references.notes: Essential collection of essays examining the 1898 Wilmington coup from multiple perspectives — political, economic, racial, and community. Includes analysis of the coup's planning, execution, and long-term consequences for Black Wilmington and North Carolina's political landscape.

source_references.author: LeRae Umfleet
source_references.title: A Day of Blood: The 1898 Wilmington Race Riot
source_references.notes: The most comprehensive single-volume account of the 1898 events, based on the North Carolina General Assembly's 2006 commission report. Umfleet's work reconstructs the massacre day by day, names victims and perpetrators, and documents the aftermath including the permanent displacement of over 2,000 Black residents.

source_references.author: Beverly Tetterton
source_references.title: Wilmington: Lost but Not Forgotten
source_references.notes: Visual and narrative history of Wilmington's demolished and vanished neighborhoods, buildings, and landmarks. Particularly valuable for documenting Brooklyn and other Black neighborhoods destroyed by urban renewal, with photographs and maps that locate communities no longer visible in the present-day cityscape.

source_references.author: Alan D. Watson
source_references.title: Wilmington, North Carolina, to 1861
source_references.notes: Scholarly history of Wilmington from its colonial founding through the antebellum era, covering the development of the port economy, the naval stores trade, the cotton export business, and the social structure of a Southern port city with a large free Black and enslaved population.
